A lot of peer storage people, our CEO had four IPOs, and everyone who's working at Augment from the research side or the engineering side, the difference I would say is they're not just a bunch of, you know, super smart college, like this is our first dive into an enterprise and building a company.

Everyone's come from like your FANG, from your snowflakes and Databricks, so they've seen how to scale enterprises and they understand all the stuff that's necessary for enterprise coding specifically.
So when you look at how these first-gen AI for code tools like Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot are architected, they take your user prompt and they do some basic content gathering.

They'll do like a grep search, Cursor does Merkle trees, Windsurf does like AST, so essentially off-the-shelf algorithms where they think that, you know, context gathering with RAG and these basic algorithms is all you need.
And then they have the user...
The model that they want to use at the end, and they use the same prompt for whatever model, assume that it's going to incrementally give you a better output.
What we figured out is LLMs are great at generating output, but it's a garbage in, garbage out problem.
So we're focusing on the input layer, and this is how Augment was essentially architected.
So we're a full GCP stack built to be a proper SaaS offering.
We didn't fork the IDE, so we're not limited to storing embeddings on a machine, and it allows us to do a few things.
So one is, you know, developers don't have to switch their battle-hardened environments.
JetBrains, VS Code, and now Vim were supported as a plugin.
And because we built out this SaaS infrastructure, we can do a hyper-scale real-time index.
So some of our biggest customers, have about 400,000 500,000 files open in one workspace simultaneously.
And we actually re-indexed at every keystroke, and we're able to take all of
Files into context.
The crux of the entire operation is really this context engine.
This is what differentiates Augment for a few reasons.
So instead of doing those basic searches, we've built a host proprietary sort of secret sauce, you might call it.
And we don't treat code as flat text.
We understand that TypeScript is different from JavaScript, which is different from Java.
So, you know, for marketing terms, we understand the shape of code.
And because of that, we can look at things like interdependencies, nuances, your SQL, your schemas, your test cases, libraries.
And on top of that, there's no model picker as well, which you've seen, Kirkland.
That's not the developer's problem, right?
There's new models coming out all the time.
How are you supposed to keep that mental map of, if I'm doing Kotlin, I'm going use this thing, and then I need to figure out pricing?
What we actually do is we gather all the right data.
We're the quality prompt, or and we'll...
We route between multiple models depending on the task at hand.
So we actually have over half a dozen models.
Some are proprietary, some are open source, and there's Frontier that we fine-tune and post-train.
And so for completions, you want that snappiness and quickness in response, so we'll go through smaller models.
For chat, we have your more complex use cases, your architecture discussions.
We'll go through larger modalities to give you that verbosity and specificity in response.
Like NextEdit itself, there's three models that go through NextEdit.
There's like an inference model, there's a description generation model, and then there's the output model as well.
So we're going to do that intelligent routing and, you know, take the onus off the developer and let them do what they do best, which is coding.
This also applies to our agent mode.
So we have native integrations via OAuth, we have MCP support, and, you know, Cursor, when served, they have a nice little UX, which is why they forked the code, but we figured it out.
Like, now we integrate natively with the terminal and VS Code and JetBrains.
We can do...
search, URL fetching.
And so we're kind of at par.
And the beauty of, you know, putting in all this work to do that SaaS infrastructure is the ability to do an actual platform play, right?

So when it comes to agents, there's this incorrect notion in the world where people think an agent will replace an entire part of the SDLC, like I'm going to have an agent for the create phase or the operate phase.
What we figured out is agents are really good at certain tasks in the SDLC.

Now, things like Cursor, Windsor, and Copilot, because they're limited to storing embeddings on a machine, they're a fork, all they can really do is this sort of, you know, create and test phase and plan phase.
Augment's set up to do phases across the entire SDLC because we can abstract that context engine.
So we have some customers we're playing around with having a sidecar for workspace context where they have an API.
We're thinking about, you know, having a merge review bot.
We're thinking about A2A protocols.

this is kind of going in with our remote agents, right?

So I'll highlight a couple of other technical separators.
One is when it comes to security.
So security-wise, we had SOC 2 Type 2.
We just got ISO 42001.
So if you're not aware, that's the new standard of measuring how not just data is handled, but how AI systems are configured and the data between them is handled.
So what a lot of people don't understand is outside of just your frontier models, you have a lot of sub-processors in some sort of AI setup.
So like Cursor, Windsurf, have like 25 sub-processors with very vague statements on what data is going to be looked at, how it's going to be trained or not trained.
And the whole thing is, oh, well, the embeddings are stored locally.
Actually, what happens is all the code...
And data is sent to the sub-processes in the cloud where the embeddings are calculated, and then it's stored on a local machine.
Our belief is embeddings can't be reverse-engineered, which, you know, there's been a lot of theoretical papers saying that it definitely can.
Instead, we're like, yeah, we scan your code, those embeddings are stored in the cloud, but what we did is we just made it super secure.
So we have customer-managed encryption keys, we have the ISO 4201, we never try on any customer data or code.
So that reduces, you know, IP leakage, that reduces data pollution.
And then we also have what's called proof of possession, which serves two purposes.
One is quality and speed, and also security.
So the way it works is, like, Kirkland, you and I say we're working on the same branch.
We both pull down the code into our workspace.
Now, if you pull it down first, Augment's going to scan the files, send it to the cloud for indexing and storing the embeddings.
If I come in secondly, Augment's to realize I'm in the same tenant, I don't need to do duplication, so I'm not going to...
So we reduced the amount of copies of code that is out there, and we reduced the index timing for every subsequent user.
Now, say you and I are working on the same file, we make different changes in our workstation.
Augment's going to assign each of us unique hashes.
So all the suggestions coming in are relevant to me and what I'm doing, and it's not going to conflict with what's going on on your side.
And that's that proof-of-possession architecture that we built.
Yeah.

Like, the best way I could say it is, one, we're set up to, you know, focus on the individual developer by giving the best IDE experience by understanding these enterprise code bases.
They're large, they're messy, there's a lot of nuance, and for that, you need a few things.
One is that hyperskill index, and two is a context engine that's able to gather the right data, not the most data.
It's not about the size of the context window, it's truly about getting the right context.
So that's a differentiator when it comes to just producing quality code.

We also have what's called recency information, I don't know if you've heard of this, where, say you're writing code, it's not just going to keep giving you the same sort of suggestions, it's going to understand the state of code.
So if right now, string equals ABC, I'm just going to realize that it was a string equals AB two iterations before.
So the more you write, the more it actually starts adapting to how you perform.
Yeah, so it's not just, you know, looking at context as a whole.
It's actually looking at what the user is doing continuously.
With that recency information, it will adapt to suggestions accordingly as well.
Yeah, I mean, I've been really impressed at what it's been able to do for me, you know, like I said, in a day and a half, basically.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Deep dive into Augment Code's technical differentiators and product roadmap](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Augment focuses on enterprise-grade coding assistance, emphasizing large, complex codebases and full SDLC support](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=351.0)
  - [Key differentiators: hyper-scale real-time indexing, proprietary context engine, multi-model routing, and remote agents](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=391.0)
  - [Future direction: Expanding beyond IDE to become a comprehensive coding awareness platform across the entire SDLC](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=604.0)

## Topics

### Augment's Technical Architecture

  - [Full GCP stack built as a proper SaaS offering, not limited to local machine storage](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=440.0)
  - [Hyper-scale real-time index capable of handling 400k-500k files simultaneously](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=467.0)
  - [Proprietary context engine understands code shape, interdependencies, and nuances](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=488.0)
  - [Multi-model routing system using 6+ models (proprietary and open-source) for different tasks](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=544.0)

### Key Features and Differentiators

  - [Native integrations with JetBrains, VS Code, Vim, and terminal](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=453.0)
  - [Agent mode with auto and manual options, task lists, and parallel processing](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=678.0)
  - [Remote agents for cloud-based code execution and PR reviews](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1035.0)
  - [Augment Memories for persistent context across agent threads](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=938.0)
  - [Prompt enhancer to improve user input quality](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=998.0)

### Security and Compliance

  - [SOC 2 Type 2 and ISO 42001 certified](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1216.0)
  - [Customer-managed encryption keys and proof-of-possession architecture](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1252.0)
  - [No training on customer data or code to prevent IP leakage and data pollution](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1265.0)

### Future Roadmap and Vision

  - [Expanding beyond individual developers to support entire SDLC](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1542.0)
  - [Potential for business logic to code translation](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1590.0)
  - [Dynamic deployments with event listeners and webhooks for continuous agent processes](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1388.0)
  - [Aiming to become the de facto coding awareness platform across the SDLC](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1620.0)

### Target Audience and Use Cases

  - [Organizations with 100-5,000 developers](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1680.0)
  - [Expanding value to non-developers (e.g., product managers, marketers) interacting with code](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1688.0)
  - [Solving complex enterprise challenges like onboarding, large migrations, and code upgrades](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1860.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Anshuman to share additional materials and slides for the GrowthX team](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=2041.0)
  - [GrowthX to review recording and materials for deeper understanding](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=2026.0)
  - [Explore marketing strategies targeting developers and potential expansion to non-developer technical roles](https://fathom.video/share/4St6Exr2kwn23-z3xkQxjb5po6KLuToe?tab=summary&timestamp=1719.0)

action_items.description: Send detailed materials (slides/docs) on Augment features/differentiators to George
